title: CCPA/CPRA — Sensitive PI — Racial or Ethnic Origin Attributes in Model Training Feature Stores
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the CCPA/CPRA programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with CCPA/CPRA control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CCPA/CPRA §1798.121 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.121 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.4.11: CCPA/CPRA — Sensitive PI — Racial or Ethnic Origin Attributes in Model Training Feature Stores.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.4.11' running on index=ml sourcetype="feast:feature_view" (view_name, feature_name, source_table, created_by, _time), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'CCPA/CPRA — Sensitive PI — Racial or Ethnic Origin Attribute' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper CCPA/CPRA control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the CCPA/CPRA control objective.

app: HTTP Event Collector from MLOps metadata service (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=ml sourcetype="feast:feature_view" earliest=-30d
| where match(feature_name,"(?i)race|ethnic|religion|union|orientation|immigration|citizen")
| stats dc(view_name) as views, values(feature_name) as features by source_table, created_by
| sort - views
description: Use of sensitive categories for automated decisioning is tightly constrained. Auditing feature-store schemas and ETL logs for prohibited attribute names or proxy variables supports demonstrable limits of processing for sensitive PI.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for CCPA/CPRA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes ml and sourcetypes feast:feature_view. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Expand keyword list with privacy counsel; (2) join `source_table` to approved sensitive-processing register; (3) block CI deploys on match in pre-prod mirror; (4) quarterly model governance review; (5) document lawful basis where processing is permitted. Indexes required: `index=ml`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=feast:feature_view`.
visualization: Table (feature views), Bar chart (by owner), Single value (distinct views).

references.title: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) — Full Text

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorized privacy team operations** — The privacy operations team performs routine data subject request processing, deletion verifications, and privacy impact assessments that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These are documented in the approved CCPA workflow procedures and should be excluded by the privacy-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled data retention processing** — Automated data lifecycle management jobs execute scheduled deletions and anonymisation runs as part of the approved data retention policy. These produce expected data processing signals that are pre-approved by the Chief Privacy Officer.

3. **Third-party service provider integrations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate data processing under written agreements (CCPA service provider contracts) generate cross-boundary data flows that may trigger detection rules during normal operations.

4. **Consumer-initiated account management** — Consumers exercising self-service account preferences, privacy settings changes, and opt-out selections through the official web portal create processing events that align with normal business operations under the CCPA framework.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `ccpa_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved service accounts, scheduled job identifiers, and known service provider IP ranges. The correlation search applies a left join against this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception has not expired.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `ml` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§1798.121`. The control objective is: "Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.121 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.4.11: CCPA/CPRA — Sensitive PI — Racial or Ethnic Origin Attributes in Model Training Feature Stores." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `ml` under `feast:feature_view`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Expand keyword list with privacy counsel. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** join `source_table` to approved sensitive-processing register. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 3:** block CI deploys on match in pre-prod mirror. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** quarterly model governance review. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** document lawful basis where processing is permitted. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `dc()`, `values()` across the last 30 days, grouped by `source_table, created_by`. For "CCPA/CPRA — Sensitive PI — Racial or Ethnic Origin Attributes in Model Training Feature Stores," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the CCPA/CPRA control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. `dc()` (distinct count) answers 'how many unique X' — use this when the control measures coverage or diversity, not raw activity volume. A million events from one user counts as 1 distinct user; this is usually the right compliance semantics. `values()` collects distinct values into a multivalue field per group; `list()` preserves order and duplicates. For compliance evidence, `values()` is preferred — the auditor cares what values appeared, not how many times.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `source_table, created_by` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(source_table) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.4.11: CCPA/CPRA — Sensitive PI — Racial or Ethnic Origin Attribute]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.4.11,reg=CCPA/CPRA
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=ml sourcetype=feast:feature_view earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `created_by`, `features`, `source_table`, `views`. Run:

```spl
index=ml sourcetype=feast:feature_view earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table created_by, features, source_table, views
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.4.11: CCPA/CPRA evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`feature views`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Distribution (`by owner`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`distinct views`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'CCPA/CPRA current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**CCPA/CPRA-specific failure modes:**

• Consumer Request categories: CCPA distinguishes Right to Know, Right to Delete, Right to Opt-Out, Right to Correct, Right to Limit. Detections on 'consumer request' must specify category — the 45-day clock applies differently to each.

• Verifiable Consumer Requests: the consumer identity must be verified 'to a reasonable degree.' Detections measuring request-to-response latency must start the clock at verification complete, not at request receipt — this is a common source of false SLA failures.

• Sale vs share distinction: CPRA broadens 'sale' to include 'share' for cross-context behavioural advertising. Detections on consent-to-share must use the current law wording; a CCPA-only detection misses the CPRA expansion.

• Do Not Sell / Do Not Share signal (GPC): Global Privacy Control must be honoured. Detections must treat GPC-asserting browsers as opted-out irrespective of UI consent state — the technical signal is authoritative.
grandmaExplanation: We use of sensitive categories for automated decisioning is tightly constrained.
